---
title: "Landsat: Earth's Longest Observation Record"
subtitle: "CASA0023 Week 2 — Sensor Presentation"
author: "CASA0023"
institute: "UCL CASA"
date: "2026"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    lib_dir: libs
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: "16:9"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
options(htmltools.dir.version = FALSE)
```

---
class: inverse, center, middle

# Landsat
### Earth's Longest Earth Observation Record

CASA0023 · UCL CASA · 2026

---
class: inverse, center, middle

# Summary

---
# Summary — What is Landsat?

Landsat is a joint **NASA/USGS** programme providing the longest continuous record of Earth's land surface from space — operating since **1972** (Wulder et al., 2022).

.pull-left[

**Programme overview**
- 9 satellites launched; **Landsat 8 and 9** currently operational
- Combined **8-day revisit** at the equator
- **30m** spatial resolution (optical), **100m** (thermal)
- Free and open data since **2008**
- Over **10 million scenes** in archive

**Landsat 8/9 bands**
- 11 bands: visible, NIR, SWIR, thermal (TIRS)
- Unique advantage: **two thermal infrared bands** — the only free sensor with this capability
- Sentinel-2 has no thermal band at all

]

.pull-right[

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.cap="Landsat 8 false colour composite. Red = vegetation, grey-blue = urban. Source: NASA/USGS (public domain)"}
knitr::include_graphics("https://eoimages.gsfc.nasa.gov/images/imagerecords/87000/87640/iran.false.color.lrg.jpg")
```

]

---
# Summary — Resolution and Limitations

**Four resolution trade-offs:**

| Resolution | Landsat 8/9 | Implication |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| Spatial | 30m optical / 100m thermal | Mixed pixels in dense urban areas |
| Spectral | 11 bands incl. TIRS | Richer than Sentinel-2 but fewer bands than hyperspectral |
| Temporal | 16 days (8 days combined) | Gaps for time-sensitive monitoring; cloud cover worsens this |
| Radiometric | 12-bit (4096 levels) | High precision; Collection 2 Level-2 products are analysis-ready |

**Key limitation:** Landsat is a **passive optical sensor** — it depends on reflected solar radiation. Cloud cover and darkness make imagery unusable during storm events, precisely when monitoring may be most needed. This is why Landsat is increasingly used alongside active SAR sensors like Sentinel-1 that are cloud-independent.

---
class: inverse, center, middle

# Applications

---
# Application 1 — Urban Heat Islands

MacLachlan et al. (2021) used Landsat 8 LST time series to map urban heat island (UHI) intensity across multiple cities, demonstrating that impervious surface expansion directly drives surface temperature increases.

.pull-left[

The study illustrates Landsat's key strength for UHI work: its **thermal infrared band** enables land surface temperature retrieval, while its **archive depth** allows detection of multi-decade warming trends that no other free sensor can match.

**Limitation noted by the study:** Landsat captures daytime LST only (~10:00 local time), missing the nocturnal UHI which is often more intense. MODIS provides both day and night acquisitions but at 1km — too coarse for intra-city analysis. This trade-off between spatial and temporal resolution remains unresolved by any single free sensor.

]

.pull-right[

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.cap="Landsat 8 thermal infrared image of iceberg A-68 calving from Larsen C ice shelf, 2017. Orange = warmest surfaces. Source: NASA Earth Observatory (public domain)"}
knitr::include_graphics("https://eoimages.gsfc.nasa.gov/images/imagerecords/90000/90574/larsenC.A68.lrg.jpg")
```

]

.footnote[MacLachlan et al. (2021) DOI: 10.3389/frsc.2021.715890]

---
# Application 2 — Land Cover Change and Water

**Land cover:** Wulder et al. (2022) highlight that Landsat's 50-year archive enables detection of land change across the full Anthropocene — urban expansion, deforestation, agricultural intensification — at 30m resolution. Sentinel-2 only provides data from 2015, making it insufficient for long-term baselines.

.pull-left[

**Surface water:** Pekel et al. (2016) used the entire Landsat archive (1984–2015) to map global surface water changes, finding **90,000 km²** of permanent water lost globally — equivalent to five Lake Eries. The MNDWI index exploits Landsat's Green and SWIR bands:

$$MNDWI = \frac{Green - SWIR}{Green + SWIR}$$

]

.pull-right[

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.cap="Landsat false-colour image showing Beijing urban expansion 1978–2011. Red = vegetation, tan/brown = built-up. Source: NASA Earth Observatory (public domain)"}
knitr::include_graphics("https://eoimages.gsfc.nasa.gov/images/imagerecords/78000/78749/beijing.comparisons.crop.jpg")
```

]

.footnote[Wulder et al. (2022) DOI: 10.1016/j.rse.2022.113195 | Pekel et al. (2016) DOI: 10.1038/nature20584]

---
class: inverse, center, middle

# Reflection

---
# Reflection

Landsat's most important quality is not its spatial resolution or spectral range — it is its **temporal consistency**. A 30m pixel from 1984 and one from 2024 can be directly compared because of rigorous radiometric calibration and collection reprocessing underpinning the entire archive (Wulder et al., 2022).

This matters because the most important questions in urban remote sensing — how has land surface temperature changed as cities have grown? where has vegetation been lost? how has impervious surface expanded? — are **longitudinal questions** requiring decades of data, not a single snapshot.

At the same time, Landsat's passive nature is a genuine constraint that practical applications must plan around. The future points toward **harmonised Landsat-Sentinel-2 (HLS)** datasets that combine both missions, and further integration with Sentinel-1 SAR to address the cloud cover problem. **Landsat Next (~2030)** will improve spatial resolution to 10–20m and add 26 spectral bands, but the 50-year archive remains the programme's irreplaceable contribution.

For this module, working with Landsat data in SNAP and QGIS this week made clear that the gap between knowing what a sensor measures and being able to extract meaningful information from it is significant. The archive depth and free data policy make Landsat an obvious starting point for learning Earth observation analysis, but the processing steps required — radiometric calibration, atmospheric correction, resampling — demand careful attention before any scientific interpretation is possible.

---
# References

Masek, J.G. et al. (2020). Landsat 9: Empowering open science and applications through continuity. *Remote Sensing of Environment*, 248, 111881. DOI: 10.1016/j.rse.2020.111881

MacLachlan, A., Biggs, E., Roberts, G. and Boruff, B. (2021). Sustainable City Planning: A Data-Driven Approach for Mitigating Urban Heat. *Frontiers in Built Environment*, 6. DOI: 10.3389/frsc.2021.715890

Pekel, J.F., Cottam, A., Gorelick, N. and Belward, A.S. (2016). High-resolution global map of surface water and its long-term changes. *Nature*, 540, pp. 418–422. DOI: 10.1038/nature20584

Wulder, M.A. et al. (2022). Fifty years of Landsat science and impacts. *Remote Sensing of Environment*, 280, 113195. DOI: 10.1016/j.rse.2022.113195
